preFix.title: Routing unwanted furniture and homewares to charities and reuse schemes
preFix.subtitle: Finding the right channel for what you are removing, so it actually gets reused
preFix.excerpt: Charity shops reject most furniture and half of homewares because they lack space, testing equipment, or collection logistics. Knowing which items which charities accept, and which alternative routes exist for things they cannot take, avoids the skip and reduces the administration of trying every charity in turn.
preFix.sourceNotes: WRAP UK 'Reuse of household goods' evidence review (2022). Furniture Re-use Network guidance for donors. BCRS (Business Charity Re-use Scotland) donor guidance. Freecycle Network and Freegle usage data.

content.text: The instinct to donate furniture and homewares to charity is right, but charities are selective. High-street shops cannot take sofas, mattresses, large appliances, or flat-pack furniture. Knowing which items go where saves multiple phone calls.
Small homewares, books, clothing: high-street charity shops (Oxfam, BHF, Mind). Clean and complete items only. Call ahead in summer when stock is high; many shops limit donations then.

(FRN) member finder at frn.org.uk lists local social enterprises with collection vehicles. Most offer free collection if the item is in reasonable condition. Sofas need a fire label; post-1988 sofas are acceptable, older ones are not.

Large and small electrical appliances: British Heart Foundation Furniture and Electrical stores collect and test electrical items. Local social enterprises in the FRN sometimes do the same. WEEE recycling at the tip is the fallback, not charity donation, for appliances that do not work.

Mattresses: charities rarely accept mattresses due to hygiene restrictions. Freecycle, Freegle, and local Facebook groups work for clean mattresses. Mattress recycling companies charge 10 to 30 pounds for collection and recycling.
Building materials (tiles, timber, doors, bricks): try salvage dealers or SALVO (salvage.net). Community RePaint handles paint. Habitat for Humanity ReStores accept building materials in some areas.
Items that rarely find a reuse route

Textiles with significant wear or staining: most charities cannot resell them. Many charity shops have a rag bin that takes worn clothing for industrial reuse; use this rather than general recycling. Curtains and bedding above 10 years old rarely find a buyer; rag banks at supermarkets accept them. Broken crockery, non-recyclable plastics, and worn-out equipment go to the household recycling centre.

Homewares placed on Freecycle or Freegle move faster in spring and autumn (house moves, students) and slowly in August (holidays) and January (post-Christmas). List items before you need the space, not after. A photograph taken outdoors in natural light and accurate measurements in the listing reduce requests from people who then cannot collect. Items stored in a shed or garage attract moisture and mould; what was reusable in September may not be in February.
